---
title: "The Story of Musa Across the Quran — A Chronological Synthesis"
category: "Cross-surah narrative"
prompt: "Tell the life of Moses as one flowing story from birth to death, weaving every Quran chapter that carries part of his narrative into a single chronological prose arc. Ground each stage in tafsir from Ibn Kathir, Ibn Ashur, al-Biqa'i, and al-Tabari, and place episodes outside the timeline — like al-Khidr — as a postscript."
date: "2026-03-18"
model: "Claude Opus 4.6"
tools_used:
  - fetch_quran(28:4-5, 28:7, 28:10, 28:12-13, 28:15-16, 28:18, 28:20-21, 28:24-25, 28:29-31, ar-simple-clean)
  - fetch_quran(20:9, 20:12-13, 20:17-18, 20:39, 20:41, 20:44, 20:67-68, 20:77, 20:85-88, 20:90, 20:94-97, ar-simple-clean)
  - fetch_quran(7:109-110, 7:121-122, 7:126, 7:130-131, 7:133-135, 7:143-145, 7:150, 7:154, 7:160, 7:171, ar-simple-clean)
  - fetch_quran(26:10-15, 26:18-22, 26:38-51, 26:52-54, 26:60-63, 26:65-68, ar-simple-clean)
  - fetch_quran(10:87-92, 27:7-14, 79:15-26, 17:101-103, ar-simple-clean)
  - fetch_quran(2:49-61, 18:60-68, 18:71, 18:74, 18:77-82, ar-simple-clean)
  - fetch_translation(28:4-31, 20:9-98, 7:103-171, 26:10-68, 10:87-92, 27:7-14, 79:15-26, 17:101-103, 2:49-61, 18:60-82, en-abdel-haleem)
  - fetch_tafsir(28:7-13, 28:29-31, 20:17-18, 20:39-44, 7:143-145, 10:90-92, 18:65-66, en-ibn-kathir)
  - fetch_tafsir(28:3, 27:7, ar-tahrir-wa-tanwir)
  - fetch_tafsir(7:103, 28:4-5, ar-nathm-aldurar)
  - search_quran("Moses", "Musa", translations=auto)
---

The Quran tells the story of Musa (Moses) in over a dozen surahs — more than any other prophet. No two accounts are identical. Each selects, amplifies, or omits different episodes depending on the surah's themes, audience, and rhetorical moment. What follows traces the arc of Musa's life as a single continuous thread, weaving together each surah's distinctive contribution at the point where it becomes relevant — and noting, at each stage, what that particular telling adds that no other does.

---

## I. Birth and Infancy

The political backdrop comes from al-Qasas. Pharaoh had "made himself high and mighty in the land and divided the people into different groups: one group he oppressed, slaughtering their sons and sparing their women" ([28:4](https://quran.com/28/4)). But the Quran immediately frames what follows as a reversal: "We wished to favour those who were oppressed in that land, to make them leaders, the ones to survive" ([28:5](https://quran.com/28/5)).

:::verse
ref: 28:4-5
link: https://quran.com/28/4-5
attribution: Abdel Haleem

إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلَا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَهَا شِيَعًا يَسْتَضْعِفُ طَائِفَةً مِّنْهُمْ يُذَبِّحُ أَبْنَاءَهُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيِي نِسَاءَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ · وَنُرِيدُ أَن نَّمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوَارِثِينَ
---
Pharaoh made himself high and mighty in the land and divided the people into different groups: one group he oppressed, slaughtering their sons and sparing their women — he was one of those who spread corruption. But We wished to favour those who were oppressed in that land, to make them leaders, the ones to survive.
:::

Into this world, Musa is born. Al-Qasas gives the fullest account of what happens next. His mother receives an extraordinary instruction: "Suckle him, and then, when you fear for his safety, put him in the river: do not be afraid, and do not grieve, for We shall return him to you and make him a messenger" ([28:7](https://quran.com/28/7)). According to **Ibn Kathir**'s commentary on these verses, Musa was born during a year when Pharaoh's agents were actively killing male children. His mother placed him in a box-cradle and set it in the river; the current carried it to Pharaoh's own household.

:::verse
ref: 28:7
link: https://quran.com/28/7
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰ أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ ۖ فَإِذَا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِي الْيَمِّ وَلَا تَخَافِي وَلَا تَحْزَنِي ۖ إِنَّا رَادُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ وَجَاعِلُوهُ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ
---
We inspired Moses' mother, saying, "Suckle him, and then, when you fear for his safety, put him in the river: do not be afraid, and do not grieve, for We shall return him to you and make him a messenger."
:::

What al-Qasas captures better than any other surah is the emotional texture. The day after she casts him into the river, "Moses' mother felt a void in her heart — if We had not strengthened it to make her one of those who believe, she would have revealed everything about him" ([28:10](https://quran.com/28/10)). She sends his sister to follow at a distance. The baby refuses every wet-nurse — **Ibn Kathir** explains that "because of his honored status with Allah, it was forbidden by divine decree" that anyone but his own mother nurse him. The sister intervenes: "Shall I tell you about a household which could bring him up for you and take good care of him?" ([28:12](https://quran.com/28/12)). And so, through this chain of seeming accidents, Musa is returned to his own mother's arms: "We restored him to his mother in this way, so that she might be comforted, not grieve, and know that God's promise is true" ([28:13](https://quran.com/28/13)).

:::verse
ref: 28:10
link: https://quran.com/28/10
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَأَصْبَحَ فُؤَادُ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰ فَارِغًا ۖ إِن كَادَتْ لَتُبْدِي بِهِ لَوْلَا أَن رَّبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قَلْبِهَا لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
---
The next day, Moses' mother felt a void in her heart — if We had not strengthened it to make her one of those who believe, she would have revealed everything about him.
:::

:::verse
ref: 28:13
link: https://quran.com/28/13
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَرَدَدْنَاهُ إِلَىٰ أُمِّهِ كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
---
We restored him to his mother in this way, so that she might be comforted, not grieve, and know that God's promise is true, though most of them do not know.
:::

**Taha** tells the same story in a radically different way. There is no narrated scene — instead, God speaks directly to Musa years later, compressing his entire infancy into a single address of overwhelming intimacy: "I showered you with My love and planned that you should be reared under My watchful eye" ([20:39](https://quran.com/20/39)). The sister, the river, the refusal to nurse — all reduced to a few phrases in God's own voice, as if to say: I was orchestrating every moment, and I want you to know it now.

:::verse
ref: 20:39
link: https://quran.com/20/39
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّي وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلَىٰ عَيْنِي
---
I showered you with My love and planned that you should be reared under My watchful eye.
:::

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the birth

Al-Qasas gives us a mother's anguish, a sister's courage, and the architecture of providence working through vulnerable women. Taha gives us God's retrospective tenderness — the same events, seen from the divine vantage.
:::

---

## II. The Killing and the Flight to Midian

Only al-Qasas narrates this episode in full. "When Moses reached full maturity and manhood, We gave him wisdom and knowledge" ([28:14](https://quran.com/28/14)). Then the fatal moment: he enters the city, finds an Israelite fighting an Egyptian, intervenes, and strikes the Egyptian dead. His immediate response is repentance: "This must be Satan's work … Lord, I have wronged myself. Forgive me" ([28:15-16](https://quran.com/28/15-16)). The next day, the same Israelite calls for his help again, and Musa realizes the pattern: "You are clearly a troublemaker" ([28:18](https://quran.com/28/18)). But a man runs from the city: "Moses, the authorities are talking about killing you, so leave" ([28:20](https://quran.com/28/20)). And so Musa flees: "My Lord, save me from people who do wrong" ([28:21](https://quran.com/28/21)).

:::verse
ref: 28:15-16
link: https://quran.com/28/15-16
attribution: Abdel Haleem

قَالَ هَٰذَا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطَانِ ۖ إِنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ مُّضِلٌّ مُّبِينٌ · قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي فَغَفَرَ لَهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ
---
He said, "This must be Satan's work: he is clearly a misleading enemy." He said, "Lord, I have wronged myself. Forgive me," so He forgave him; He is truly the Most Forgiving, the Most Merciful.
:::

He walks toward Midian. At the well, he sees two women holding their flocks back while the men water theirs. He helps them. He withdraws into the shade and prays one of the most human prayers in the entire Quran: "My Lord, I am in dire need of whatever good thing You may send me" ([28:24](https://quran.com/28/24)). One of the women returns — "walking shyly" ([28:25](https://quran.com/28/25)) — and her father offers Musa a marriage contract: eight years of service, or ten if he chooses. Musa accepts.

:::verse
ref: 28:24
link: https://quran.com/28/24
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَسَقَىٰ لَهُمَا ثُمَّ تَوَلَّىٰ إِلَى الظِّلِّ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لِمَا أَنزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ
---
He watered their flocks for them, withdrew into the shade, and prayed, "My Lord, I am in dire need of whatever good thing You may send me."
:::

**Al-Shu'ara** doesn't narrate any of this — but it surfaces later in Pharaoh's courtroom as an accusation. Pharaoh throws it in his face: "And then you committed that crime of yours: you were so ungrateful" ([26:19](https://quran.com/26/19)). Musa's reply is remarkable for its candor: "I was misguided when I did it … and I fled from you in fear; later my Lord gave me wisdom and made me one of His messengers" ([26:20-21](https://quran.com/26/20-21)). And then the devastating reframe: "And is this — that you have enslaved the Children of Israel — the favour with which you reproach me?" ([26:22](https://quran.com/26/22)).

:::verse
ref: 26:20-22
link: https://quran.com/26/20-22
attribution: Abdel Haleem

قَالَ فَعَلْتُهَا إِذًا وَأَنَا مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ · فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِي رَبِّي حُكْمًا وَجَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ · وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَيَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ
---
He said, "I was misguided when I did it, and I fled from you in fear; later my Lord gave me wisdom and made me one of His messengers. And is this — that you have enslaved the Children of Israel — the favour with which you reproach me?"
:::

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the flight

Al-Qasas gives the full human narrative — fear, guilt, flight, loneliness, marriage, belonging. Al-Shu'ara shows the same past weaponized by Pharaoh and then reframed by Musa as the journey that led him to prophethood.
:::

---

## III. The Fire and the Divine Call

All three surahs describe the same event — Musa, travelling with his family after completing his term in Midian, sees a fire on the mountainside — but each frames the encounter differently.

**Al-Qasas** sets the scene most precisely: "Once Moses had fulfilled the term and was travelling with his family, he caught sight of a fire on the side of the mountain" ([28:29](https://quran.com/28/29)). **Ibn Kathir** explains that it was a cold, dark, rainy night and they had lost their way. He tried to start a fire and failed. Then he saw the distant blaze. When he reaches it, the voice calls: "Moses, I am God, the Lord of the Worlds" ([28:30](https://quran.com/28/30)). He is told to throw his staff; it becomes a snake; he flees; he is called back: "Draw near! Do not be afraid" ([28:31](https://quran.com/28/31)). Then the white hand, and the commission to go to Pharaoh.

**Taha** opens with the most direct address: "Has the story of Moses come to you?" ([20:9](https://quran.com/20/9)). Its version of the fire is the most intimate of the three. There is a remarkable exchange: "Moses, what is that in your right hand?" God asks ([20:17](https://quran.com/20/17)). Musa answers with endearing specificity: "It is my staff. I lean on it; restrain my sheep with it; I also have other uses for it" ([20:18](https://quran.com/20/18)). **Ibn Kathir** notes that some scholars read this question as God drawing Musa's attention to what is about to be transformed — establishing the ordinariness of the staff before demonstrating the extraordinariness of God's power. Then: "I am your Lord. Take off your shoes: you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa. I have chosen you, so listen to what is being revealed" ([20:12-13](https://quran.com/20/12-13)). And later: "I have chosen you for Myself" ([20:41](https://quran.com/20/41)). According to **Ibn Kathir**, even Adam in his dialogue with Musa acknowledged this unique status: "Are you the one whom Allah chose for His Message, and He selected you for Himself?"

:::verse
ref: 20:12-13
link: https://quran.com/20/12-13
attribution: Abdel Haleem

إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ فَاخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ بِالْوَادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى · وَأَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ فَاسْتَمِعْ لِمَا يُوحَىٰ
---
"I am your Lord. Take off your shoes: you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa. I have chosen you, so listen to what is being revealed."
:::

Taha also uniquely includes God's command about how to approach Pharaoh: "Speak to him gently so that he may take heed, or show respect" ([20:44](https://quran.com/20/44)). **Ibn Kathir** comments: "Even though Fir'awn was the most insolent and arrogant of people and Musa was the friend of Allah among His creation at that time, Musa was still commanded to speak to Fir'awn with mildness and softness."

:::verse
ref: 20:44
link: https://quran.com/20/44
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَقُولَا لَهُ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا لَّعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشَىٰ
---
"Speak to him gently so that he may take heed, or show respect."
:::

**Al-Naml** is briefer but adds its own notes. Musa tells his family: "I have seen a fire. I will bring you news from there, or a burning stick for you to warm yourselves" ([27:7](https://quran.com/27/7)). The voice at the fire declares: "Blessed is the person near this fire and those around it; may God be exalted, the Lord of the Worlds" ([27:8](https://quran.com/27/8)) — a formulation unique to this surah. It also explicitly numbers the signs: "These are among the nine signs that you will show Pharaoh" ([27:12](https://quran.com/27/12)).

:::verse
ref: 27:7-8
link: https://quran.com/27/7-8
attribution: Abdel Haleem

إِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَهْلِهِ إِنِّي آنَسْتُ نَارًا سَآتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ آتِيكُم بِشِهَابٍ قَبَسٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ · فَلَمَّا جَاءَهَا نُودِيَ أَن بُورِكَ مَن فِي النَّارِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ
---
Moses said to his family, "I have seen a fire. I will bring you news from there, or a burning stick for you to warm yourselves." When he reached the fire, a voice called: "Blessed is the person near this fire and those around it; may God be exalted, the Lord of the Worlds."
:::

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the divine call

Al-Qasas gives the physical setting and the fear. Taha gives the theological intimacy — shoes removed, chosen for God Himself, commanded to speak gently. Al-Naml gives the blessing of the ground itself and the numbering of the signs.
:::

---

## IV. The Confrontation with Pharaoh

The courtroom confrontation is the episode most frequently retold, and each version serves a different rhetorical purpose.

**Al-Shu'ara** is almost entirely dialogue — the most dramatic, rapid-fire exchange. Musa's vulnerability is front and center: "My Lord, I fear they will call me a liar, and I will feel stressed and tongue-tied, so send Aaron too; besides, they have a charge against me, and I fear they may kill me" ([26:12-14](https://quran.com/26/12-14)). God answers simply: "No. Go, both of you, with Our signs — We shall be with you, listening" ([26:15](https://quran.com/26/15)).

:::verse
ref: 26:12-15
link: https://quran.com/26/12-15
attribution: Abdel Haleem

قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ · وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِي وَلَا يَنطَلِقُ لِسَانِي فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَارُونَ · وَلَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنبٌ فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ · قَالَ كَلَّا ۖ فَاذْهَبَا بِآيَاتِنَا ۖ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ
---
He said, "My Lord, I fear they will call me a liar, and I will feel stressed and tongue-tied, so send Aaron too; besides, they have a charge against me, and I fear they may kill me." He said, "No. Go, both of you, with Our signs — We shall be with you, listening."
:::

Before Pharaoh, the theological debate is sharpest in al-Shu'ara. Three times Musa defines God differently — "Lord of the heavens and earth" ([26:24](https://quran.com/26/24)), "your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers" ([26:26](https://quran.com/26/26)), "Lord of the East and West" ([26:28](https://quran.com/26/28)) — while Pharaoh escalates from mockery ("This messenger who has been sent to you is truly possessed" — [26:27](https://quran.com/26/27)) to naked threat ("If you take any god other than me, I will throw you into prison" — [26:29](https://quran.com/26/29)).

**Al-A'raf** gives the most expansive confrontation narrative, with more attention to the political machinery. It is Pharaoh's advisors who drive the plot: "This man is a learned sorcerer! He means to drive you out of your land!" ([7:109-110](https://quran.com/7/109-110)). Pharaoh turns to them: "What do you suggest?" ([7:110](https://quran.com/7/110)) — revealing a ruler who rules by committee and fear.

**Taha** adds the psychological detail that "Moses was inwardly alarmed" ([20:67](https://quran.com/20/67)) when the sorcerers' ropes and staffs seemed to move — a note of vulnerability found only here — followed by God's reassurance: "Do not be afraid, you have the upper hand" ([20:68](https://quran.com/20/68)).

**Al-Nazi'at** compresses the entire confrontation into twelve verses, stripping it to its theological core. God calls, Musa offers purification and guidance, Pharaoh refuses and declares "I am your supreme lord" ([79:24](https://quran.com/79/24)), and God condemns him. The surah ends: "there truly is a lesson in this for anyone who stands in awe of God" ([79:26](https://quran.com/79/26)). No staff, no plagues, no sea — just the essential choice.

:::verse
ref: 79:23-26
link: https://quran.com/79/23-26
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَحَشَرَ فَنَادَىٰ · فَقَالَ أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ · فَأَخَذَهُ اللَّهُ نَكَالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَالْأُولَىٰ · إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ
---
He summoned his people and proclaimed, "I am your supreme lord," and so God condemned him to punishment in the last life and in the first. There truly is a lesson in this for anyone who stands in awe of God.
:::

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the confrontation

Al-Shu'ara gives the rhetoric and the dialogue. Al-A'raf gives the political machinery. Taha gives the inner fear. Al-Nazi'at gives the parable stripped bare.
:::

---

## V. The Sorcerers' Conversion

All three surahs tell this episode, and each develops the sorcerers' transformation with distinct emphasis.

In all three, the sorcerers fall to their knees after Musa's staff devours their trickery, and they declare: "We believe in the Lord of the Worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron" ([7:121-122](https://quran.com/7/121-122); [26:47-48](https://quran.com/26/47-48)). In Taha, the wording is slightly different: "We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses" ([20:70](https://quran.com/20/70)) — Aaron named first, perhaps because Pharaoh had just accused Musa of being their "master."

:::verse
ref: 26:46-48
link: https://quran.com/26/46-48
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ سَاجِدِينَ · قَالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ · رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ
---
The sorcerers fell down prostrating themselves, and said, "We believe in the Lord of the Worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron."
:::

Pharaoh's response is identical in structure across all three: fury, accusation of conspiracy, and the threat of crucifixion. But each adds details. In al-Shu'ara, the sorcerers respond with extraordinary courage: "That will do us no harm … for we are sure to return to our Lord. We hope that our Lord will forgive us our sins, as we were the first to believe" ([26:50-51](https://quran.com/26/50-51)). In Taha, they say: "We shall never prefer you to the clear sign that has come to us, nor to Him who created us. So decide whatever you will: you can only decide matters of this present life" ([20:72](https://quran.com/20/72)). In al-A'raf, their prayer is most poignant: "Our Lord, pour steadfastness upon us and let us die in devotion to You" ([7:126](https://quran.com/7/126)).

:::verse
ref: 26:50-51
link: https://quran.com/26/50-51
attribution: Abdel Haleem

قَالُوا لَا ضَيْرَ ۖ إِنَّا إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ · إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَايَانَا أَن كُنَّا أَوَّلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
---
They said, "That will do us no harm: we are sure to return to our Lord. We hope that our Lord will forgive us our sins, as we were the first to believe."
:::

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the conversion

Al-Shu'ara gives their defiance and hope. Taha gives their philosophical clarity — this life versus the next. Al-A'raf gives their prayer for steadfastness.
:::

---

## VI. The Plagues and Signs

Only al-A'raf narrates the plagues in detail. First comes the broader punishment: "We inflicted years of drought and crop failure on Pharaoh's people, so that they might take heed" ([7:130](https://quran.com/7/130)). Their response reveals their psychology: "when something good came their way, they said, 'This is our due!' When something bad came, they ascribed it to the evil omen of Moses" ([7:131](https://quran.com/7/131)).

Then the specific plagues, compressed into a single sweeping verse: "We let loose on them the flood, locusts, lice, frogs, blood — all clear signs. They were arrogant, wicked people" ([7:133](https://quran.com/7/133)). And a devastating cycle: "whenever a plague struck them" they would beg Musa to pray for relief and promise to believe and release the Israelites, "but when We relieved them of the plague … they broke it" ([7:134-135](https://quran.com/7/134-135)).

:::verse
ref: 7:133
link: https://quran.com/7/133
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ الطُّوفَانَ وَالْجَرَادَ وَالْقُمَّلَ وَالضَّفَادِعَ وَالدَّمَ آيَاتٍ مُّفَصَّلَاتٍ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا وَكَانُوا قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ
---
We let loose on them the flood, locusts, lice, frogs, blood — all clear signs. They were arrogant, wicked people.
:::

Al-Isra adds a distinctive note: "We gave Moses nine clear signs" ([17:101](https://quran.com/17/101)), and Pharaoh's taunt: "Moses, I think that you are bewitched" — to which Musa replies: "You know well that no one but the Lord of the heavens and the earth has sent these as eye-opening proofs. I truly think, O Pharaoh, that you are indeed doomed" ([17:101-102](https://quran.com/17/101-102)).

:::verse
ref: 17:101-102
link: https://quran.com/17/101-102
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ آيَاتٍ بَيِّنَاتٍ ۖ فَاسْأَلْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِذْ جَاءَهُمْ فَقَالَ لَهُ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يَا مُوسَىٰ مَسْحُورًا · قَالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا أَنزَلَ هَٰؤُلَاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصَائِرَ وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يَا فِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُورًا
---
We gave Moses nine clear signs — ask the Children of Israel. When Moses came to them, Pharaoh said to him, "Moses, I think you are bewitched." He said, "You know well that no one but the Lord of the heavens and the earth has sent these as eye-opening proofs. I truly think, O Pharaoh, that you are indeed doomed."
:::

Al-Naml provides the psychological verdict found nowhere else: "They denied them, in their wickedness and their pride, even though their souls acknowledged them as true" ([27:14](https://quran.com/27/14)).

:::verse
ref: 27:14
link: https://quran.com/27/14
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا ۚ فَانظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ
---
They denied them, in their wickedness and their pride, even though their souls acknowledged them as true. See how those who spread corruption met their end.
:::

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the plagues

Al-A'raf gives the cycle of plague, plea, and broken promise. Al-Isra gives the personal exchange — Pharaoh and Musa speaking as men who know each other. Al-Naml gives the inner psychology of denial.
:::

---

## VII. The Exodus and the Parting of the Sea

The escape is told with different emphases in each surah.

**Al-Shu'ara** gives the most cinematic account. "Pharaoh and his people pursued them at sunrise" ([26:60](https://quran.com/26/60)). When the two groups come within sight of each other, Musa's followers panic: "We shall definitely be caught" ([26:61](https://quran.com/26/61)). Musa's reply is one of the most celebrated lines in the Quran: "No, my Lord is with me: He will guide me" ([26:62](https://quran.com/26/62)). Then the sea parts — "each side like a mighty mountain" ([26:63](https://quran.com/26/63)).

:::verse
ref: 26:61-63
link: https://quran.com/26/61-63
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَلَمَّا تَرَاءَى الْجَمْعَانِ قَالَ أَصْحَابُ مُوسَىٰ إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ · قَالَ كَلَّا ۖ إِنَّ مَعِيَ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ · فَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ أَنِ اضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ الْبَحْرَ ۖ فَانفَلَقَ فَكَانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ كَالطَّوْدِ الْعَظِيمِ
---
When the two sides came within sight of each other, Moses' followers said, "We shall definitely be caught." He said, "No, my Lord is with me: He will guide me." We revealed to Moses: "Strike the sea with your staff." It parted — Loss on each side like a mighty mountain.
:::

**Taha** adds God's direct instruction: "Travel by night with My servants and strike for them a dry path through the sea; you will not fear being overtaken nor be afraid" ([20:77](https://quran.com/20/77)). The detail of a *dry* path is unique to this telling.

:::verse
ref: 20:77
link: https://quran.com/20/77
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَلَقَدْ أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِي فَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقًا فِي الْبَحْرِ يَبَسًا لَّا تَخَافُ دَرَكًا وَلَا تَخْشَىٰ
---
We revealed to Moses: "Travel by night with My servants and strike for them a dry path through the sea; you will not fear being overtaken nor be afraid."
:::

**Al-Baqara**, addressing the Children of Israel directly, states it most succinctly: "We parted the sea for you, so saving you and drowning Pharaoh's people right before your eyes" ([2:50](https://quran.com/2/50)).

**Yunus** contributes the episode found in no other surah: Pharaoh's deathbed confession. As he drowns, he cries: "I believe there is no God except the one the Children of Israel believe in. I submit to Him" ([10:90](https://quran.com/10/90)). God's answer is devastating: "Now? When you had always been a rebel, and a troublemaker!" ([10:91](https://quran.com/10/91)). Then the extraordinary declaration: "Today We shall save only your corpse as a sign to all posterity" ([10:92](https://quran.com/10/92)). **Ibn Kathir** explains that some Israelites doubted Pharaoh had died, so "Allah commanded the sea to throw his body — whole, without a soul — with his known armor plate" to the shore as proof.

:::verse
ref: 10:90-92
link: https://quran.com/10/90-92
attribution: Abdel Haleem

حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَدْرَكَهُ الْغَرَقُ قَالَ آمَنتُ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُو إِسْرَائِيلَ وَأَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ · آلْآنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ · فَالْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ آيَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ النَّاسِ عَنْ آيَاتِنَا لَغَافِلُونَ
---
When he was about to drown, he said, "I believe there is no God except the one the Children of Israel believe in. I submit to Him." "Now? When you had always been a rebel, and a troublemaker! Today We shall save only your corpse as a sign to all posterity. A great many people fail to heed Our signs."
:::

Yunus also uniquely records an earlier instruction: "House your people in Egypt and make these houses places of worship; keep up the prayer" ([10:87](https://quran.com/10/87)), and Musa's prayer against Pharaoh: "Our Lord, obliterate their wealth and harden their hearts so that they do not believe until they see the agonizing torment" ([10:88](https://quran.com/10/88)).

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the exodus

Al-Shu'ara gives the drama and Musa's faith at the moment of crisis. Taha gives God's direct guidance. Al-Baqara makes it a reminder to a community. Yunus gives Pharaoh's final cry, its rejection, and the preservation of his body.
:::

---

## VIII. Mount Sinai — The Tablets and the Theophany

After the sea crossing, the narrative shifts to Sinai. Only al-A'raf narrates Musa's extraordinary request: "My Lord, show Yourself to me: let me see You!" God replies: "You will never see Me, but look at that mountain: if it remains standing firm, you will see Me." And when his Lord revealed Himself to the mountain, He made it crumble: Moses fell down unconscious" ([7:143](https://quran.com/7/143)).

:::verse
ref: 7:143
link: https://quran.com/7/143
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَلَمَّا جَاءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَاتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُ رَبُّهُ قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ لَن تَرَانِي وَلَٰكِنِ انظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكَانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرَانِي ۚ فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّىٰ رَبُّهُ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسَىٰ صَعِقًا ۚ فَلَمَّا أَفَاقَ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
---
When Moses came for Our appointment and his Lord spoke to him, he said, "My Lord, show Yourself to me: let me see You!" He said, "You will never see Me, but look at that mountain: if it remains standing firm, you will see Me." And when his Lord revealed Himself to the mountain, He made it crumble: Moses fell down unconscious. When he recovered, he said, "Glory be to You! I turn to You in repentance. I am the first of the believers."
:::

:::commentary
**Ibn Kathir** records a prophetic hadith about this moment: on the Day of Resurrection, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ said he would find Musa "holding onto a pillar of the Throne" and would not know "if he woke up before me or he received his due because of his unconsciousness on Mount At-Tur." When Musa wakes, he says: "Glory be to You! I turn to You in repentance, and I am the first of the believers" ([7:143](https://quran.com/7/143)). According to **Ibn Kathir**, Mujahid explained this repentance as being "from asking You to look at You."
:::

God then gives Musa the Tablets: "Hold unto these with firmness, and enjoin your people to take the better therein" ([7:145](https://quran.com/7/145)). **Ibn Kathir** states that the Tablets contained "advice and the details of the commandments for lawful and prohibited matters" — either the Tawrah itself or a precursor to it.

Al-A'raf also records the raising of the mountain over the Israelites: "We raised the mountain over them as if it had been a canopy, and they thought that it was going to fall on them. 'Hold firmly to what We have given you and remember that which is therein'" ([7:171](https://quran.com/7/171)).

:::verse
ref: 7:171
link: https://quran.com/7/171
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُ وَاقِعٌ بِهِمْ خُذُوا مَا آتَيْنَاكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَاذْكُرُوا مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ
---
When We raised the mountain over them as if it had been a canopy, and they thought that it was going to fall on them: "Hold firmly to what We have given you and remember what is therein, so that you may be mindful of God."
:::

Al-Baqara adds that the Israelites demanded to see God directly: "Moses, we will not believe you until we see God face to face." At that, thunderbolts struck you as you looked on. Then We revived you after your death, so that you might be thankful" ([2:55-56](https://quran.com/2/55-56)).

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to Sinai

Al-A'raf gives the theophany — the most theologically extraordinary scene in the entire Moses cycle. Al-Baqara shows the Israelites' own demand and its consequences.
:::

---

## IX. The Golden Calf

**Taha** gives the most detailed and distinctive account. It uniquely names the instigator: "the Samiri has led them astray" ([20:85](https://quran.com/20/85)). God tells Musa of the crisis before he returns, and the scene of his return is devastating: "My people, did your Lord not make you a gracious promise? Was my absence too long for you?" ([20:86](https://quran.com/20/86)). The people explain that they threw their borrowed jewellery into the fire, "and the Samiri did the same" — and from the molten gold he produced "an image of a calf which made a lowing sound, and they said, 'This is your god and Moses' god, but he has forgotten'" ([20:88](https://quran.com/20/88)).

:::verse
ref: 20:87-88
link: https://quran.com/20/87-88
attribution: Abdel Haleem

قَالُوا مَا أَخْلَفْنَا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنَا وَلَٰكِنَّا حُمِّلْنَا أَوْزَارًا مِّن زِينَةِ الْقَوْمِ فَقَذَفْنَاهَا فَكَذَٰلِكَ أَلْقَى السَّامِرِيُّ · فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُ خُوَارٌ فَقَالُوا هَٰذَا إِلَٰهُكُمْ وَإِلَٰهُ مُوسَىٰ فَنَسِيَ
---
They said, "We did not break our promise to you deliberately. We were carrying the weight of the people's jewellery, so we threw it — and the Samiri did the same" — and he produced an image of a calf which made a lowing sound, and they said, "This is your god and Moses' god, but he has forgotten."
:::

Musa's interrogation of the Samiri is found only here: "What was the matter with you, Samiri?" ([20:95](https://quran.com/20/95)). The Samiri's answer is enigmatic: "I saw something they did not; I took in some of the teachings of the Messenger but tossed them aside: my soul prompted me to do what I did" ([20:96](https://quran.com/20/96)). Musa's sentence is also unique to Taha: "Your lot in this life is to say, 'Do not touch me'" ([20:97](https://quran.com/20/97)) — a perpetual exile from human contact.

:::verse
ref: 20:95-97
link: https://quran.com/20/95-97
attribution: Abdel Haleem

قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكَ يَا سَامِرِيُّ · قَالَ بَصُرْتُ بِمَا لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِّنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِي نَفْسِي · قَالَ فَاذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِي الْحَيَاةِ أَن تَقُولَ لَا مِسَاسَ
---
He said, "And what was the matter with you, Samiri?" He said, "I saw something they did not; I took in some of the teachings of the Messenger but tossed them aside: my soul prompted me to do what I did." He said, "Go away! Your lot in this life is to say, 'Do not touch me.'"
:::

Aaron's defense is most poignant in Taha: "Son of my mother — let go of my beard and my hair! — I was afraid you would say, 'You have caused division among the children of Israel'" ([20:94](https://quran.com/20/94)).

**Al-A'raf** gives the physical rage: "He threw the tablets down and seized his brother by the hair, pulling him towards him" ([7:150](https://quran.com/7/150)). Aaron's plea is the same in substance but differently voiced: "Son of my mother, these people overpowered me! They almost killed me!" ([7:150](https://quran.com/7/150)). And then the aftermath — "when Moses' anger abated, he picked up the Tablets, on which were inscribed guidance and mercy" ([7:154](https://quran.com/7/154)).

:::verse
ref: 7:150
link: https://quran.com/7/150
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَلَمَّا رَجَعَ مُوسَىٰ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ غَضْبَانَ أَسِفًا قَالَ بِئْسَمَا خَلَفْتُمُونِي مِن بَعْدِي ۖ أَعَجِلْتُمْ أَمْرَ رَبِّكُمْ ۖ وَأَلْقَى الْأَلْوَاحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُ إِلَيْهِ
---
When Moses returned to his people, angry and aggrieved, he said, "What a foul thing you have done in my absence! Were you so keen to bring your Lord's judgment forward?" He threw the Tablets down and seized his brother by the hair, pulling him towards him.
:::

**Al-Baqara** addresses the community directly with the starkest demand: "You have wronged yourselves by worshipping the calf, so repent to your Maker and kill [the guilty among] you" ([2:54](https://quran.com/2/54)).

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the golden calf

Taha gives the Samiri, his motive, and his unique punishment. Al-A'raf gives the emotional explosion and recovery. Al-Baqara gives the severest demand for repentance.
:::

---

## X. The Wilderness Complaints

After liberation, the Quran's focus shifts to the Israelites' behavior. Al-Baqara is the primary witness, addressing them directly as "Remember when …" It catalogues a pattern of divine favor met with ingratitude: the clouds for shade, manna and quails sent down, yet "it was not Us they wronged; they wronged themselves" ([2:57](https://quran.com/2/57)). Moses strikes the rock and twelve springs gush forth ([2:60](https://quran.com/2/60)). And then the complaint: "Moses, we cannot bear to eat only one kind of food, so pray to your Lord to bring out for us some of the earth's produce, its herbs and cucumbers, its garlic, lentils, and onions" ([2:61](https://quran.com/2/61)). Musa's response is sharp: "Would you exchange better for worse?" ([2:61](https://quran.com/2/61)).

:::verse
ref: 2:60
link: https://quran.com/2/60
attribution: Abdel Haleem

وَإِذِ اسْتَسْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ فَقُلْنَا اضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ الْحَجَرَ ۖ فَانفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ اثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۖ كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا مِن رِّزْقِ اللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ
---
When Moses prayed for water for his people, We said to him, "Strike the rock with your staff." Twelve springs gushed out, and each group knew its own drinking place. "Eat and drink from God's provision and do not cause corruption in the land."
:::

Al-A'raf echoes the same events — the twelve springs, the manna and quails, the shade of clouds ([7:160](https://quran.com/7/160)) — but frames them within a narrative rather than as direct address.

:::insight
title: What each surah adds to the wilderness

Al-Baqara is admonition — a mirror held up to a community. Al-A'raf is history — the same events narrated in the third person.
:::

---

## XI. The Compressed Parable

It is worth returning to al-Nazi'at separately, because it represents something unique in the Quran's treatment of Musa: the entire confrontation reduced to twelve verses and stripped to its moral skeleton. "Have you heard the story of Moses?" ([79:15](https://quran.com/79/15)). God calls. Musa goes. He offers Pharaoh purification. Pharaoh refuses. He declares himself supreme lord. God punishes him in this life and the next. "There truly is a lesson in this for anyone who stands in awe of God" ([79:26](https://quran.com/79/26)). No plagues, no staff, no sea, no sorcerers — just the essential architecture of choice: submission or tyranny.

:::verse
ref: 79:15-19
link: https://quran.com/79/15-19
attribution: Abdel Haleem

هَلْ أَتَاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰ · إِذْ نَادَاهُ رَبُّهُ بِالْوَادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى · اذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ · فَقُلْ هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ · وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشَىٰ
---
Have you heard the story of Moses? His Lord called out to him in the sacred valley of Tuwa: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has truly exceeded all bounds. Say, 'Do you want to purify yourself? Do you want me to guide you to your Lord so that you may hold Him in awe?'"
:::

---

## Episodes Outside the Main Arc

### Musa and al-Khidr (Surah al-Kahf, 18)

This episode stands apart from every other Musa narrative. There is no Pharaoh, no Egypt, no confrontation with power. Instead, it belongs to a later period: according to the hadith recorded by **Ibn Kathir** in his commentary on [18:65](https://quran.com/18/65), "Musa got up to deliver a speech before the Children of Israel and he was asked, 'Who is the most learned person among the people?' Musa replied, 'I am.' Allah rebuked him because he did not refer the knowledge to Allah." God then told him of a servant at the junction of two seas who knew what Musa did not.

The meeting unfolds as a journey of humility. Al-Khidr warns him: "You will not be able to have patience with me. How could you be patient in matters beyond your knowledge?" ([18:67-68](https://quran.com/18/67-68)).

:::verse
ref: 18:65-66
link: https://quran.com/18/65-66
attribution: Abdel Haleem

فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَا آتَيْنَاهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَاهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا · قَالَ لَهُ مُوسَىٰ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَىٰ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا
---
They found one of Our servants, a man to whom We had granted Our mercy and whom We had given knowledge of Our own. Moses said to him, "May I follow you so that you can teach me some of the right guidance you have been given?"
:::

Three episodes follow — a damaged boat, a killed boy, a repaired wall — each provoking Musa's moral outrage. Each time the hidden wisdom is revealed. The boat belonged to poor people and was damaged to save it from a king who was seizing every sound vessel ([18:79](https://quran.com/18/79)). The boy's parents were believers, and he would have led them to transgression ([18:80-81](https://quran.com/18/80-81)). The wall concealed a treasure for two orphans, and their righteous father's legacy was the reason it was preserved ([18:82](https://quran.com/18/82)). Al-Khidr concludes: "I did not do any of this on my own authority" ([18:82](https://quran.com/18/82)).

The bird on the ship's edge dips its beak in the sea, and al-Khidr says (in the hadith): "My knowledge and your knowledge and the knowledge of all of creation, in comparison to the knowledge of Allah, is like what this bird has taken from the sea."

This is the only Quranic episode where Musa is not the teacher but the student — and it teaches that even the knowledge of a prophet who spoke directly with God is a drop beside the divine ocean.

---

## What Emerges: The Composite Portrait

No single surah contains the "full" story. Each is a lens, and together they build a portrait of extraordinary depth.

Musa is at once a baby floating in a river under God's watchful eye ([28:7-13](https://quran.com/28/7-13), [20:39](https://quran.com/20/39)), a frightened young man who killed someone and fled ([28:15-21](https://quran.com/28/15-21)), a husband lost on a cold night trying to find fire for his family ([28:29](https://quran.com/28/29)), a prophet who stutters and begs for his brother's help ([26:12-14](https://quran.com/26/12-14)), a man commanded to speak gently to the most powerful tyrant on earth ([20:44](https://quran.com/20/44)), a leader who faces down that tyrant with nothing but a staff and the words "my Lord is with me" ([26:62](https://quran.com/26/62)), a seeker of God so bold he asks to see the Divine and is overwhelmed ([7:143](https://quran.com/7/143)), a man so furious at his people's betrayal that he throws down sacred tablets and grabs his brother by the hair ([7:150](https://quran.com/7/150)), and a student humble enough to follow a stranger on a journey he doesn't understand ([18:66-70](https://quran.com/18/66-70)).

The Quran's method is not repetition — it is refraction. The same life, passed through different thematic prisms, reveals different dimensions: providence, divine intimacy, political courage, the rhetoric of truth against power, the limits of human knowledge, the psychology of denial, and the unending tension between gratitude and ingratitude.

Taken together, Musa emerges as the most fully human prophet in the Quran — not despite his flaws and fears, but through them.

:::sources
- Abdel Haleem, *The Qur'an: A New Translation* (en-abdel-haleem)
- Ibn Kathir, *Tafsīr al-Qurʾān al-ʿAẓīm* (en-ibn-kathir)
- Ibn Ashur, *al-Taḥrīr wa-l-Tanwīr* (ar-tahrir-wa-tanwir)
- Al-Biqa'i, *Naẓm al-Durar* (ar-nathm-aldurar)
- Al-Tabari, *Jāmiʿ al-Bayān* (ar-tabari)
:::

:::grounding
fetch_quran(28:4-5, 28:7, 28:10, 28:12-13, 28:15-16, 28:18, 28:20-21, 28:24-25, 28:29-31, 20:9, 20:12-13, 20:17-18, 20:39, 20:41, 20:44, 20:67-68, 20:77, 20:85-88, 20:90, 20:94-97, 7:109-110, 7:121-122, 7:126, 7:130-131, 7:133-135, 7:143-145, 7:150, 7:154, 7:160, 7:171, 26:10-15, 26:18-22, 26:38-51, 26:52-54, 26:60-63, 26:65-68, 10:87-92, 27:7-14, 79:15-26, 17:101-103, 2:49-61, 18:60-68, 18:71, 18:74, 18:77-82, ar-simple-clean), fetch_translation(same ranges, en-abdel-haleem), fetch_tafsir(28:7-13, 28:29-31, 20:17-18, 20:39-44, 7:143-145, 10:90-92, 18:65-66, en-ibn-kathir), fetch_tafsir(28:3, 27:7, ar-tahrir-wa-tanwir), fetch_tafsir(7:103, 28:4-5, ar-nathm-aldurar)
:::
